paragraphs.body: (Intro cut for time – they’re sitting on a hotel balcony playing card games)
Cal groans, scrubbing a hand over his face to hide his grimace.

In some dust-laden, untouched crevice within him, Cal truly does appreciate Sasha’s attempts to lighten the mood. On any other day, he would’ve let himself be drawn into their usual back and forth ribbing. Of all the friends he’s had – which isn’t really a sizable group for comparison – Sasha is the easiest to talk to. He’s got no qualms about speaking his mind, but has enough sense to tread lightly when Cal’s trying to hide an open wound that both of them know is there. It’s a kindness Sasha rarely extends, an abrupt diversion from their typical dynamic that sets Cal’s teeth on edge. He doesn’t want to be treated like a fabergé egg right now. He needs to be shattered into a thousand pieces and sifted through in search of salvageable parts.
The rest of their game is a swift mercy killing. Sasha has never gone easy on him and he thankfully doesn’t start now, even as he flicks thinly-veiled glances of concern at Cal.

Standing, Cal collects the cards and jams them back into their box, pretends he’s not a sore loser, and shivers as another bead of sweat slithers down his spine. This late in the summer, the air is usually mild, but tonight it’s dense and viscous. Warm wind swirls at their ankles like stagnant pond water disturbed by wandering feet, algae clinging to legs in the form of sticky sweat splotches. Far off the shoreline of nearby Lake Erie, even farther off than the train, a storm lingers.

“I think I’m gonna head to bed, ‘m tired. You?” Sasha asks through a yawn, arms stretched up over his head. Cal nods, wordlessly, and turns to tug the sliding door open and head inside. A blast of cool air greets them, courtesy of their free hotel air conditioning which Sasha had cranked down to the lowest possible setting like the thermostat czar he is.

annotations.comment: I can picture this so well.

paragraphs.body: Sasha sets to work on getting ready for bed, but Cal lingers by the door. It’s been one tight space after another these past few days: from his tiny studio in Columbus to the even tinier passenger seat of Sasha’s sedan to the probate court, which wasn’t tiny but still suffocated him. After years of pre-law studies, he’d been more than prepared to sit in a court of law, but it was disorienting to be on the other side of the proceeding - that, compounded by the bouts of dizzying head-rushes every time his aunt was referred to as the ‘decedent’, made for a long afternoon.
Instead of following Sasha’s lead, he brushes past the two twin beds to the small entryway, jamming his feet into shoes and swiping a key card off the television stand.
“Need to stretch my legs,” He tells Sasha, “I won’t be long.”

Sasha stares at him for a moment, expressionless, as if he’s looking right through Cal to the beige wall behind him. Cal stares back, challenging. He wants Sasha to say something. To confront him so he can lash out, tear and eviscerate until Sasha realizes that he’s in over his head, that this is too much trouble to go to for a former college roommate turned friend. Then Sasha can pack his things and leave Cal to lick his wounds in mortifying solitude.
Sasha doesn’t do any of those things. He only gives a small jerk of the head and a clipped, “Okay.”

Something thrums under Cal’s skin. Even though he knows Sasha is not planning on stopping him, he stomps out anyway, the click of the closing door ringing like a gunshot in the empty corridor. The buzz of fluorescent lights follows him from the hotel’s carpeted halls to the stairwell and out into the parking lot, street lamps mimicking the drone of cicadas and the copper glow of candlelight as he passes under them. Just ahead, the storm beckons and warns in tandem.

annotations.comment: Your expansion of this moment is really nice.

paragraphs.body: No cars are out, but he skitters across the road anyway, sandals slapping in an almost comical way as he climbs over the rocky border between asphalt and sand. At high tide, there’s only a sliver of dry sand, littered with rough debris, to stand on. The rest of the “beach”, if you can call it one, only appears between gushes of black water that spit out offerings in the form of stones and shards of seashells. These semi-beaches are scattered all over Port Clinton, squashed between marinas and lakeside restaurants that become eerily empty as the days grow shorter and colder.

He frequented a beach just like this one, not too far from here where it sits opposite the apartment complex he grew up in. His aunt Sloane didn’t like him riding his bike down to the bigger, realer beach just a few streets away. Not since his classmate Nicole Moore had been caught in a car versus bike hit-and-run and only survived up till the hospital, where she died as the first patient in Sloane’s overnight shift. Cal doesn’t know the details: he would never ask because he knows Sloane would tell. But she had come into his room that morning after, staring at him from the doorway as he pretended to sleep, and when he feigned a headache to get out of school, she let him stay home without question. That was all he needed to know, really.

annotations.comment: I wonder if he realized this at the time or only later.

paragraphs.body: The beach feels all at once familiar yet foreign. He’s stood here a thousand times before, with his back to the apartment and eyes on the waves. Except he hasn’t, because this beach is miles away from there and much rockier, and the apartments he was raised in have since been converted to vacation rentals.

He plunks down on a somewhat flat rock, heedless of the rough sand and prickly vegetation, drops his head into his hands, and cries. A little at first - hot tears that wet the seams of his eyelids, a few solitary drops making their way down the valley of his face - then more, and more, until he’s no longer crying. Just gasping for air in between wet hacks, trying to expel something that will never be dislodged.

Nicole Moore, the beach before him, Sloane, the apartment he was raised in – they’re all gone. Tomorrow he will follow his usual visiting routine: he’ll wake up at the crack of dawn, take the ferry to an island in the middle of Lake Erie, find his way to the bright yellow house where Sloane is supposed to be. But once he’s inside that house, he’ll spend his day floating between empty rooms and haunting doorways because Sloane isn’t there and she won’t be anywhere ever again.

annotations.comment: I think this is really close to what you want. I still worry that the way you’ve written it implies that the loss of Nicole Moore and the loss of his aunt are roughly equal to Cal, but I know that’s not what you mean.
annotations.context_left: will follow his usual visiting routine: he’ll wake up at the crack of dawn, take the ferry to an isl
annotations.context_right: in the middle of Lake Erie, find his way to the bright yellow house where Sloane is supposed to be.

paragraphs.body: More wet drops fall to his face, but this time, they’re coming from the sky. Cal stumbles down from his perch, almost braining himself in his attempts to scramble back up to the road before hustling back to the hotel. The storm is faster though, catching up to him in the parking lot and pelting him with cold pricks of rain that sting at his arms and legs but soothe his puffy face. By the time he reaches their room, he’s breathless and cold, fingers trembling as he attempts to slide the keycard into the lock. He gets it eventually, popping the door open and sliding inside to an unexpectedly bright room.

annotations.comment: I wondered if he was going to try to pull off stoicism as he enters.
annotations.context_left: cks of rain that sting at his arms and legs but soothe his puffy face. By the time he reaches their
annotations.context_right: , he’s breathless and cold, fingers trembling as he attempts to slide the keycard into the lock. He

paragraphs.body: Sasha is sitting up in bed across the room with a book propped against his knees. His eyes slide over to Cal with that same expressionless face, and Cal crumples. Somewhere between his ass hitting the floor and the burning rush of humiliation at the nape of his neck, Sasha had gotten out of bed to come sit on the floor with him, bony hand pulling Cal’s head to rest on Sasha’s shoulder. Neither of them are huggers – Cal isn’t sure if this even counts as a hug – but, all things being equal, it’s not the worst.
He swallows back his shame, but the guilt still festers. Sasha shouldn’t be seeing this.
“She’s dead,” He whispers, drunk on a cocktail of emotions, into the thin cotton of Sasha’s sleep shirt. “I didn’t see her for- I won’t see her again.”
“No,” Sasha says, and Cal doesn’t know if it’s an agreement or disagreement.
The ferry ride across Lake Erie from Port Clinton to South Bass Island is, on average, only twenty minutes long. Today, it felt like an eternity.

When Cal had lived with his aunt Sloane, it was in a one-bedroom apartment on the east side of Port Clinton, where they had to install a divider down the middle of the bedroom and a murphy bed in the living room for when Sloane’s snoring got to be too much for Cal to sleep through. It was only after his second year away at college that she bought the house on South Bass, quitting her job as a pharmacy tech at the hospital so she could channel all her energy into “creating, as God intended” - and create she did. During Cal’s last visit, it took them the better part of an hour to clear a space for him in her spare bedroom, the room fit to burst with the number of canvases she’d stuffed in there, each covered in varying degrees of paint. Most were works in progress: sunrises with no earth to emerge from, figures with no faces, boats with no waves to carry them, faces with no figures. Only a few were completed, signature and all. Of those, only one was hung up on the wall.

annotations.comment: I don’t have a lot of comments here because I’m enjoying the reading. My only note is to wonder whether some part of this might be better conveyed in conversation with Sasha.

paragraphs.body: He can picture it easily. Besides being the only piece of Sloane’s art on the walls, it’s also hung on the wall opposite the staircase, so you can’t even avoid seeing it as you walk to the second floor. In it, a woman, perhaps around Cal’s age, sits cross-legged in a sea of brush-stroked grass, leaning back on her hands with her face tipped up towards an unseen sun. It’s bright in her world, so much so that she has to squeeze her eyes shut to reveal the beginnings of crow’s feet near her temples, though her lips remain stretched out in a close-mouthed grin. Her head lolls to one side, towards the viewer, though the rest of her body is angled away.

annotations.comment: Slightly confusing — his age now, or his age when he saw it?

paragraphs.body: Cal hasn’t seen the painted woman or her creator in over three months. He tells himself he was too busy, between wrapping up his studies, house-hunting, and searching for a job while also window shopping for law schools - but that’s only a half truth. He had time, there’s always time, but the other half of the truth is a far more difficult pill to swallow.

annotations.comment: I’m torn here. I enjoy the suspense it creates, but the narrative stance is so close to Cal’s thoughts that it does feel a little like cheating not to tell us the rest.

paragraphs.body: When the ferry finally docks, Cal takes his time herding Sasha off the boat, letting most of the top deck clear out before making their way downstairs. He makes Sasha go first, warily eying the way his entire body sways out of sync with the rocking of the ship - Cal’s not small by any means, but next to Sasha, anyone would look slightly.

annotations.comment: Helpful perhaps to let us know that this is Sasha’s first trip here?

paragraphs.body: They manage to disembark without any major injuries, though the steep hill up to the road and golf cart rental almost finishes Cal off. He tries not to let the attendant see how winded he is as they make their way through the rental agreement, promising the girl behind the counter that no, he will not operate the cart while under the influence and yes, he will be the only driver. Once he’s signed the dotted line and they’ve found their cart, well out of the attendant’s line of sight, Cal tosses Sasha the keys and plunks down into the passenger seat.

annotations.comment: Why not just have Sasha rent the cart?

paragraphs.body: “You know how this works?” He glances at Sasha’s side profile. His thick brows are furrowed at the set of instructions printed on the wheel, finger tracing along as he mouths the words to himself.
“Yes, yes,” Sasha huffs, “I can drive anything.”

Cal holds his hands up in mock surrender, lets Sasha fuss some more, then braces himself against the cracked leather seat as they go lurching out of the parking lot. With the end of tourist season drawing closer and closer, they don’t pass many people out on the road. Cal points to signs and landmarks as they go, giving Sasha the abridged tour guide version of the island. It’s hard when he barely knows the place himself, but Sasha nods along all the same. They buzz down empty stretches of pavement, feeling like the loudest thing around for miles, and pass by the airport and a campground before Cal signals Sasha to turn down a gravel lane. Lemon yellow siding soon comes into view - Sloane trimmed back much of the foliage that’d been snaking up all sides of the house - and when the golf cart comes to a stop behind the other car parked there, he can hear the clinking of a half-dozen wind chimes he knows are adorning the back deck.

annotations.comment: I find myself wanting a bit more from Sasha or at least a bit more insight into why he has little to say.

paragraphs.body: Part of Cal wants to stay right here, skin glued to the seat, and wait for Sloane to come sweeping out of the house to greet him. He wants her to fold him up in her arms again, let her smear paint on his clothes and try to rub it off with a licked thumb. He does not want to meet the owner of the unfamiliar car parked here. He technically already has, albeit it was only a clipped conversation via phone.

annotations.comment: Just judging from the rest of this paragraph, I think you mean “Cal wants to…” instead.

paragraphs.body: Besides Cal, Aileen Halloran was the only other person named in Sloane’s will, the beneficiary of a few shares of stock Cal didn’t know Sloane had as well as an inheritor of a chunk of Sloane’s extensive art collection. After the probate court notified her, she got in touch with Cal to sort out the sordid details. To say it was a short conversation would be putting it lightly. By Cal’s estimate, she said all of ten words and half of them were just ‘okay’.

annotations.comment: Here, too, a chance to have some dialogue instead of purely internal narration.
annotations.context_right: the only other person named in Sloane’s will, the beneficiary of a few shares of stock Cal didn’t k

annotations.comment: I think this is a joke but I’m not entirely sure who’s making it.
annotations.context_right: . To say it was a sh

paragraphs.body: And now, here she is, her brick red hatchback sitting where Cal usually parks and likely somewhere inside, snooping through Sloane’s life. He almost wants to wring Sloane’s neck just as much as he wants to hug her: blindsiding him with her death was one thing, but throwing in some woman he’s never met, much less heard of, before in his entire two plus decades of knowing his aunt, without so much as a head’s up is a whole other can of worms. It’s infuriating and totally par for the Sloane course.

annotations.comment: So, maybe Cal called her?
annotations.context_right: e she is, her brick red hatchback sitting where Cal usually parks and likely somewhere inside, snoop

annotations.comment: Not to keep pushing dialogue at you but having Cal say “Ugh, I could kill her” (re: his aunt) and having Sasha ask why could be very effective here.

paragraphs.body: Rounding the corner of the detached garage, Cal nearly walks head-first into Sasha’s back, who’s come up short and staring at something on the front step. Cal follows his gaze only to find the painted lady sitting out on the step. Except she’s not in canvas form anymore - a real, flesh and blood woman is looking back at them and while her crow’s feet have deepened and there’s graying around her temples now, it is unmistakably her. Her expression tries to relax into something neutral, but the stiff set of her shoulders and tightly clasped hands suggest otherwise.
One at a time, Cal forces his legs to take him up the walkway and meet the painted lady in a quick but firm handshake.
“I’m, uh, Callum,” He says, “We spoke on the phone-”

“I remember,” She cuts him off. Her voice is low, and bit rougher now than it was over the phone, as if she hadn’t spoken aloud since their last conversation. “Aileen,” She gestures to herself, then peers around Cal at Sasha’s looming form.
“Oh, right,” He clears his throat and shuffles to the side to let Sasha step forward, “this is Sash-”
“Aleksandr,” Sasha interjects.
Cal slides his eyes between the two of them as they shake hands, “Yeah, Aleksandr. He’s a friend, just here to give us an extra set of hands.”
She gives a slow nod, though her eyes narrow a fraction at Sasha, “Right.”

annotations.comment: This feels so loaded, but I don’t know why.

paragraphs.body: There’s a prolonged pause, all of them looking at one another and simultaneously anywhere but one another, before Sasha reminds him that he has the house keys. He’s relieved at the opportunity to get out from under her unnerving gaze as he bounds up the stairs to the front door. With the storm door propped open against his back, he works the keys into the locks and pops the leaf green door open for Aileen and Sasha to follow him in.

It’s the second time he’s been in here since Sloane’s passing, and the first time with company. Even Aileen’s impassive expression cracks a bit as they walk down the hall to the kitchen and living area, where every available surface has been decorated and adorned with artwork, both of Sloane’s own creation along with pieces she’s collected from second-hand stores and flea markets.

Cal leaves Aileen and Sasha to balk as he heads over to the space he cleared on the kitchen table. The packet he’d found when he first came back to the house sits right where he left it, the letter from the beginning torn out and folded up in his suitcase back at the hotel. The rest of it contains pages upon pages of listed artworks, each with a set of initials next to them: ‘CD’ for Cal, ‘AH’ for Aileen. As executor of the will, Cal thinks he should be able to chuck this list in the bin and duke it out with Aileen himself. He can’t, because the state of Ohio is, and has always been, against him, but he wants to, as selfish as it is. He’s also curious, though, to see what made Aileen special enough to not only get an inclusion in the will, but a permanent space nailed onto Sloane’s wall.

annotations.comment: Gawk?

My read on Cal is that it’s a little churlish not to want her to have some small portions of the estate. I get that he’s grieving, but I’m not quite following why sharing a little bit of his aunt’s things is so upsetting to him.

paragraphs.body: Clearing his throat, he stands to pass along the packet to Aileen, who begins flipping through the pages with pursed lips and stony eyes.
“A lot of it is in the spare bedroom upstairs,” Cal points a thumb at the ceiling, “But there’s a lot on there that I don’t recognize. Some of it’s probably out in the garage, too.”

Aileen hums, but her eyes don’t lift from the paper. Another long pause, then she flicks the packet shut and spins it back around to him. “Well, let’s get at it, then.” And with that, she hands the packet back and marches off towards the stairs.
“Well she’s a real peach,” Sasha hums under his breath.
Cal’s eyes roll back into his head. “Listen, I don’t care what ‘bad energy’ you’re picking up right now, but you need to stow it, alright? Not everyone is out to get you.”
It’s a bit harsh, but Sasha looks properly chastised, so Cal counts it as a win.

annotations.comment: It’s a little funny that Cal dislikes her so much but is made at Sasha for disliking her.
annotations.context_left: It’s a bit harsh, but S
annotations.context_right: ha looks properly chastised, so Cal counts it as a win.

paragraphs.body: “How about this,” Cal says, at normal volume now, “How about you go start on the garage and I’ll go upstairs and help Aileen.” He tears off the last page of the packet and passes it to Sasha, who obeys without a word by disappearing down the hall.

Cal makes his way over to the stairs but stutters to a stop when he sees the back of Aileen’s form, motionless, standing at the top of the stairs. He waits a moment, then pretends to cough before loudly proceeding up the stairs in the hopes that Aileen will be startled into motion. She is not.

From a few steps back, Cal follows her gaze onto the wall where the painted lady lives. He hadn’t considered the possibility that Aileen had never seen the painting, nor at the very least heard of it, but that possibility is starting to look more and more real with each passing second that Aileen continues to stare.
“That was her favorite piece, I think,” Cal feels compelled to say, “It’s the only bit of her own work that she ever hung up. Only piece she ever thought was good enough to hang up.”

annotations.comment: Has Cal softened toward Aileen here?

paragraphs.body: Aileen turns her head back to him, but her body remains angled away. “How long,” She starts, pausing when her voice comes out weak and choked, clearing her throat before continuing, “How long’s it been there?”

He has to think for a moment. As long as he’s been coming here, he thinks. As long as Sloane has called this place home. Ever since she left Port Clinton to live the life she always wanted, the life she’d postponed to raise Cal.
He only relays that first thought to Aileen, which makes her look away again. They sit in silence for many more seconds.

“She left it for you,” Cal speaks up again, because the silence is eating him alive. He passes the packet up to her with his finger pointing to the title of the painting with a bold AH neatly printed on the end. Aileen scans it once before shoving the packet back into his chest.
“I don’t want it,” She says, then knocks shoulders with him as she pushes her way back downstairs.

“What am I supposed to do with it?” He calls after her, then, against his better judgment, moves to follow. He doesn’t want it either, but he also doesn’t feel comfortable dumping it in some thrift store drop-off bin or tossing it in the trash.

annotations.comment: Why doesn’t eh want it? If he doesn’t want it why is he so annoyed about giving it to her?

paragraphs.body: “Figure it out!” She yells back. It’s the loudest she’s been all day.

Cal catches up to her in the driveway, gravel crackling underfoot skids to a stop in front of her car. She’s gotten into the driver’s seat but hasn’t closed the door, and the blank expression she’s been trying to pass off as normal all day is finally cracking.

“Look, I’m sorry about whatever,” he makes some wild gesture in the general direction of the house, “just happened, but we’re kind of on a time crunch and it would really help if we could just get this over with sooner rather than later.” Her face twists in confusion. The car chimes along in the background, upset at the door still being open while the keys are in the ignition.

“Oh I’m sorry,” she says, blunt, “Am I inconveniencing you? D’you need my help gettin’ all her junk out of the way so you can move your happy little ass in this afternoon?” Her voice is thick now, accented with something out of Appalachia that she must’ve been holding back earlier.

The back of Cal’s neck burns, out of anger and shame and the overhead sun. “No, I need to collect her things so I can get the house ready to sell. The property taxes are too high – I can’t afford to stay.” He doesn’t know if he ever wanted to keep the house, but that’s none of Aileen’s business anyhow. None of this is her business. Finally, he grits out, “So can you please, come back inside and give me a hand. Please.”

annotations.comment: I appreciate this dialogue. I wonder if he’s also on a time crunch because he has to get home, etc.?I really like her leaving. It ups the stakes.

paragraphs.body: She slams the car door shut, silencing the car’s chiming, and spits out, “I’ll be back when I’m good and ready to come back.”
Even though he already wanted her to leave, seeing her pull out of the driveway and flee down the road pisses him off all the same.

annotations.comment: You might consider having this be the moment when Cal decides he doesn’t like her, rather than earlier. This is another strong excerpt, Sophia. Your changes are effective and the story is moving along nicely. My biggest note for you is to allow your characters to change in the scenes — you have a tendency (and it’s not at all uncommon to do this in a draft) to decide Cal feels a certain way and assert that, only to have him actually arrive at that feeling within the scene. I also think you’re not using Sasha enough — he’s there. Let them talk. Strong work! I’m excited to see how this continues to develop.
